summary: Logan County Commissioners voted Feb 18, 2026 to approve new data center regulations, lifting the 6-month moratorium imposed in Oct 2025. The regulations govern data center facilities, battery energy storage systems, and solar facilities. Granite Renewables is proposing a $15B, 2-gigawatt data center on 4,198 acres of privately owned land 21 miles northwest of Sterling. During public hearings, numerous citizens raised concerns about property rights, water and electrical usage, and impacts on the culture and heritage of Logan County. The Logan County Economic Development Corporation supported the project but the community remains divided.

Colorado House Bill 1030 offers a 20- to 30-year, 100% sales and use tax exemption on qualified purchases for data center builders. Nonpartisan legislative staff estimate the bill would cut state general fund revenue by at least $29 million annually. A broad coalition of environmentalists and fiscal groups opposes the bill, warning energy-hungry data centers could inflate electricity bills and push Colorado's climate targets further out of reach. The bill competes with SB 26-102 which would regulate data centers without offering tax incentives. As of mid-March 2026, both bills remain under legislative consideration.

project_name: HB 874 Data Center Electricity Rate Protection
summary: Rep. Stephanie Mickelsen (R-Idaho Falls) introduced HB 874, aimed at preventing data centers or other new large power users (20+ MW) coming online in Idaho from increasing utility rates for other customers. The bill would prevent the Idaho Public Utilities Commission from approving any new customer requiring 20 megawatts or more of electricity without first proving it would not require raising rates for existing customers. This follows the 2025 session where similar legislation passed the House 60-8 but stalled in the Senate.

Kuna farmer Layne Thornton and two business entities behind Toledo Dairy filed a petition for judicial review against the City of Kuna over the city's April 2025 approval of a 620-acre data center rezone (Gemstone Technology Park) by Kansas-based Diode Ventures. The 3-2 vote, with Mayor Joe Stear casting the tie-breaking vote, rezoned farmland owned by Duane Yamamoto near Locust Grove and Barker Roads. The lawsuit alleges City Council Member Greg McPherson should have recused himself due to being employed by the plaintiff at the time of the vote. Separately, Kuna Council President Chris Bruce opposed the project due to its location near farmland and homes, far from the town's industrial zone.

Eleven data center developers are now in talks with NorthWestern Energy, according to environmental groups who obtained the information. By 2030, NorthWestern could supply 1,400 MW to data centers, roughly equivalent to the electricity needs of more than 1 million homes and more than double the 759 MW that NorthWestern's existing customers require on a typical day. A coalition including Big Sky 55+, Butte Watchdogs, Climate Smart Missoula, Helena Interfaith Climate Advocates, Honor the Earth, MEIC, and NW Energy Coalition is organizing opposition statewide, with standing-room-only town halls in Billings, Butte, Broadview, and Great Falls. Residential and small business customers (98% of NorthWestern's customer base) will likely pay for infrastructure unless protections are put in place.
opposition_groups: Butte Watchdogs for Social and Environmental Justice

summary: Eagle Mountain City Council tabled proposed amendments to the RTI Overlay Zone on Jan 20, 2026, opting for additional review and public input. The amendments would allow energy generation projects including small modular nuclear reactors to power the city's growing data center operations, after Google delayed building a campus due to Rocky Mountain Power's inability to meet energy demand. The Planning Commission had recommended denial 3-2. A Change.org petition titled 'Stop the Proposed Code Changes to Allow Nuclear Reactors in Eagle Mountain' collected signatures from residents concerned about radioactive storage. Resident Joy Rasmussen said: 'It means our city would become a radioactive storage site.' The city made two prior attempts in 2025 to adopt the ordinance, both of which failed after mixed feedback.

Utah HB 76, sponsored by Rep. Jill Koford (R-Ogden), passed both chambers and requires new data centers (10,000+ sq ft using 75+ acre-feet of water annually) to report water usage to the State Engineer for public compilation. Noncompliance carries $10,000/day fines. The bill initially failed in the Senate by two votes before being amended to apply only to new data centers. The Utah Rivers Council's Alta Fairbourne argued 'there's no reason why we should be incentivizing data centers to come to our state in this time of water crisis.' Gov. Spencer Cox has until March 26 to sign or veto. Law takes effect in May if signed.

Lyon County Board of Commissioners approved 4-1 (Commissioner Hockaday opposed) a Master Plan Amendment rezoning 505 acres from Agriculture to Specific Plan for the $11B Monarch Data Center in Mason Valley, 10 miles north of Yerington. The 14-building, 4.6M sq ft complex would require 1,000 MW of power. At the Dec 4 meeting, 13 members of the public spoke against and 4 in favor. Mason Valley is one of Nevada's most overtapped groundwater basins where farmers already face water cuts. A local rancher warned: 'The zoning is getting passed really fast, there's no environmental assessments, and long term feasibility and community impacts are not being addressed.' The Pyramid Lake Paiute Tribe also expressed concern that water diversions would reduce flows to Pyramid Lake.

Sierra Club Toiyabe Chapter filed an appeal of the Reno Planning Commission's Dec 2024 approval of the 82,000 sq ft Webb Data Center in the North Valleys — the first data center within Reno city limits. The Sierra Club argued the approval was made via an administrative decision with no public notice and that data centers are 'unique in their impact to residential communities, the electric grid, and natural resources use.' Adding controversy, former city manager Doug Thornley, who resigned in July 2024, later represented the Webb Data Center as a private attorney at Holland & Hart. Reno City Council held a hearing on Jan 22, 2025 but ultimately denied the Sierra Club's appeal, allowing the project to proceed.

Project Jupiter faces growing community opposition as it awaits two air quality permits from the NM Environment Department. Over 5,200 public comments were submitted on the permit applications, with 1,500+ from Sierra Club supporters opposing air pollution. If approved, the gas-powered microgrids could emit 14M+ tons of greenhouse gases annually — more than Albuquerque and Las Cruces combined. Environmental advocates say developers appear to be splitting emissions across two permits to avoid 'major source' classification. A secretive ad campaign by 'Project Jupiter Together' has been promoting the project statewide. The NM Environmental Law Center has filed two separate lawsuits: one alleging commissioners violated the Open Meetings Act, another claiming they voted on an incomplete bond application. Project Jupiter is now shaping the 2026 Dona Ana County commissioner elections.

NM state legislator proposed more oversight and regulations for data center 'microgrids' — the behind-the-meter natural gas power plants that projects like Project Jupiter plan to build to generate their own electricity independently of the local grid. The bill responds to concerns that such microgrids circumvent utility regulation and public accountability for emissions. Sen. Jeff Steinborn and environmental groups argue that private power plants for massive data centers will worsen regional air quality and threaten emission reduction goals.
